articles.title: about this blog
articles.contents:
Hello.
I’m currently a PhD student in the Department of Sociology at Duke University.
My research interests have to do with culture, economic sociology, and network theory.
I’m also interested in contributing to the type of quantitative research that fights against this common stereotype:



Currently thinking about:
When government by abstractions works and when it fails (e.g. Scott 1998; Stinchcombe 2001).
How political elites shape belief networks.
The evolution of legal precedent
birth, life-expectancy, and “death” of precedents
influence, innovation, and competition

Factor analysis and other dimensionality reduction techniques.
Networks of social support (e.g. Small 2017), kinship, and gender.



Scott, James C. 1998. Seeing Like a State: How Certain Schemes to Improve the Human Condition Have Failed. New Haven: Yale University Press.


Small, Mario Luis. 2017. Someone to Talk to. Oxford University Press.


Stinchcombe, Arthur L. 2001. When Formality Works: Authority and Abstraction in Law and Organizations. University of Chicago Press.
